channel_name: This Week in Startups · Jason Calacanis
video_title: The dawn of surgery bots + buy a home for $250 (w/ Andromeda & Mogul) | E2313
gist: Andromeda Surgical CEO Nick Damiano describes building autonomous AI-driven surgical robots starting with a urology procedure (HoLEP), highlighting a 45-case data moat and $15M capital-efficient path to launch, while Mogul CEO Alex Blackwood details a fractional single-family rental investing platform with $250 minimums, 8-12% dividend yields, and plans for a secondary market.

insights.claim: Andromeda Surgical CEO Nick Damiano argues surgery today is 'almost untouched by AI' but expects AI and autonomy to become ubiquitous in surgical practice within roughly a decade, comparing the field's current state to the horse-and-buggy era of driving versus where autonomous cars like Tesla and Waymo are now.
insights.speaker: Nick Damiano, CEO of Andromeda Surgical
insights.so_what: Signals a long-run structural shift in surgical labor and medtech investment toward AI-driven robotics; investors should track early autonomous-surgery players before the category matures.
insights.quote: surgery is currently almost untouched by AI, but in 10 years AI and autonomy will be ubiquitous

insights.claim: Andromeda's current surgical robot is controlled via an iPad app running proprietary autonomy software paired with an off-the-shelf robotic arm, deliberately avoiding custom hardware so the company can focus engineering resources on the software/autonomy layer rather than building tools.
insights.speaker: Nick Damiano
insights.so_what: Using off-the-shelf hardware versus vertically integrated robotics lowers capex and speeds iteration -- a capital-efficiency thesis investors can apply when evaluating other robotics startups.
insights.quote: There's there's this arm from Cuka that we use right now. They're a German company that makes robot arms for different use cases

insights.claim: Andromeda chose HoLEP (a laser prostate-reduction procedure for BPH) as its market-entry wedge because urologists were historically the earliest and most tech-forward adopters of surgical robots (pioneered by Intuitive Surgical's da Vinci), and because HoLEP has an unusually wide skill gap between novice surgeons (roughly 5 hours per case) and expert surgeons (as fast as 30 minutes), creating clear room for an AI-assisted skill-leveling product.
insights.speaker: Nick Damiano
insights.so_what: Suggests a repeatable go-to-market playbook for surgical-AI startups: target sub-specialties with high surgeon variance and existing robot-adoption culture rather than broad platforms.
insights.quote: you've got this huge gap between somebody who's just learning, who takes like five hours and struggles through a procedure, and then the best in the world who's doing it in 30 minutes easily

insights.claim: Andromeda has already shipped early autonomy features including real-time surgical landmark tracking ('Google Maps for the body') and auto-pivoting control of the endoscope that automatically minimizes tissue-damaging force, with new software features released weekly based on live clinical cases.
insights.speaker: Nick Damiano
insights.so_what: A weekly ship cadence in a historically slow-moving medtech category is a differentiator investors can benchmark against incumbents' multi-year release cycles.

insights.claim: Damiano's long-term vision for surgery is a 'sue surgeon' model where a single surgeon oversees roughly ten simultaneous autonomous procedures from an air-traffic-control-style command position rather than performing one surgery at a time, expanding surgeon throughput rather than eliminating surgeons.
insights.speaker: Nick Damiano
insights.so_what: If realized, this multiplies effective surgeon supply many-fold, which could reshape healthcare labor economics and hospital staffing models -- a theme relevant to healthcare-labor and medtech investors.
insights.quote: a surgeon sitting at kind of an air traffic control center where there's a lot of different procedures happening

insights.claim: Andromeda's biggest bottleneck is proprietary surgical data (robot kinematics, applied forces, tissue interaction) that cannot be scraped from the web or bought like typical enterprise AI training data; the company has performed only 45 total surgical cases to date, which Damiano says constitutes the entire world's dataset for this specific autonomous procedure.
insights.speaker: Nick Damiano
insights.so_what: Real-world procedure volume, not model architecture, is the moat in physical-AI/robotics categories -- investors should weight case-volume and clinical access as heavily as software talent when evaluating surgical-AI startups.
insights.quote: it's the only data set of 45 cases that exist. Like that's 100% of the world's data

insights.claim: Andromeda supplements its own clinical data by sourcing surgical videos from YouTube creators who are practicing surgeons, since kinematic and force data must be collected in-clinic but visual/procedural technique data can partly be gathered from existing public surgical video content.
insights.speaker: Nick Damiano
insights.so_what: Points to an underappreciated data source (creator-surgeon video libraries) that could become valuable IP or a licensing opportunity as surgical-AI demand grows.

insights.claim: Because the universe of distinct surgical tasks is finite and relatively small, Andromeda expects autonomy built for one procedure to generalize to anatomically similar procedures elsewhere in the body, letting the company scale from roughly one to two new procedures per year currently toward a stated target of ten new procedures per year.
insights.speaker: Nick Damiano
insights.so_what: A task-transfer scaling model implies accelerating procedure coverage over time rather than linear cost per new procedure -- relevant to modeling the company's total addressable market expansion.
insights.quote: because the set of surgical tasks people do now is finite and pretty small then yes you can you can generalize across procedures

insights.claim: Damiano argues autonomous surgery is structurally easier to solve than autonomous driving despite having far less training data, because the surgical environment has no adversarial actors -- unconscious patients don't move or resist -- and fewer edge cases than the open road.
insights.speaker: Nick Damiano
insights.so_what: Reframes the assumption that surgery is harder than driving to automate -- investors comparing autonomous-vehicle timelines to autonomous-surgery timelines should adjust for lower environmental complexity, even though data scarcity remains a headwind.
insights.quote: you don't have these other adversarial actors like other cars or pedestrians you have to model their behavior when you're in surgery. There's nobody else working against you

insights.claim: Andromeda's FDA strategy is to submit frequent, small incremental updates rather than large infrequent submissions, since regulators evaluate the increment over the last-cleared version; keeping a surgeon in the loop, rather than shipping a fully autonomous unsupervised system, is viewed as easing regulatory acceptance.
insights.speaker: Nick Damiano
insights.so_what: A frequent-small-submission regulatory cadence could let AI-medtech companies out-iterate incumbents who file large, infrequent submissions -- a structural speed advantage worth monitoring in FDA filings.
insights.quote: what really matters is the increment over either what they've seen before or what you last submitted

insights.claim: As of this interview, Andromeda is cleared to sell commercially only in New Zealand, is close to clearance in a few additional countries, and does not yet have U.S. regulatory clearance, despite already having signed LOIs from customers ready to pay once it can legally deploy.
insights.speaker: Nick Damiano
insights.so_what: Revenue is currently gated entirely by regulatory clearance rather than demand -- a binary catalyst investors should track (next clearance geography/date) before assuming commercial ramp.
insights.quote: we're only clear to be on the market in New Zealand right now, though we're close in a few other countries

insights.claim: Andromeda went from company founding to a launched robot in three years while spending only about $15 million total, which Damiano says makes it the fastest robotics company to reach launch and far more capital-efficient than the medtech-robotics industry norm of spending hundreds of millions of dollars pre-launch. The company is also about to announce a new funding round in the tens of millions of dollars.
insights.speaker: Nick Damiano
insights.so_what: Extreme capital efficiency versus incumbents (avoiding custom hardware, heavy automation of internal processes) suggests smaller check sizes can still produce category-competitive robotics products -- relevant comp for venture sizing in medtech robotics.
insights.quote: we were the fastest robot from zero to launch in three years and we've only spent about 15 million total

insights.claim: More than half of Andromeda's team has no prior healthcare industry experience; the co-founder previously built the first autonomous truck to drive on a U.S. freeway, and the company has hired several former autonomous-trucking colleagues as well as staff from Neuralink, making its talent base closer to an autonomous-vehicle/deep-tech company than a traditional medical device company like Intuitive Surgical.
insights.speaker: Nick Damiano
insights.so_what: Cross-pollination of autonomous-vehicle engineering talent into medtech is a hiring pattern investors can use to identify other 'AV talent diaspora' startups likely to move faster than legacy medtech incumbents.
insights.quote: more than half of the team is not from has never worked in any healthcare at all

insights.claim: Despite building an AI-autonomy company, Andromeda deliberately avoids using generative AI/LLMs within the actual surgical control product because current models are not yet reliable enough to rule out hallucination-style errors in a safety-critical setting; the current autonomy stack instead relies on the type of machine learning used in autonomous vehicles like Tesla and Waymo.
insights.speaker: Nick Damiano
insights.so_what: Counters the assumption that an 'AI company' automatically means LLM-based; investors should distinguish generative-AI hype from the perception/control AI actually being deployed in physical safety-critical systems.
insights.quote: We have to be careful in the beginning to not have things that are going to hallucinate in weird ways

insights.claim: Andromeda uses AI/LLMs extensively for internal operations such as documentation and parts of its coding workflow, but applies guardrails around AI-generated code because it can introduce nondeterministic bugs, and remains open to eventually using synthetic data or 'world model' approaches for surgical training data once those techniques mature, while acknowledging present drawbacks and risks of doing so today.
insights.speaker: Nick Damiano
insights.so_what: Signals synthetic-data/world-model techniques for physical-AI training are still pre-adoption even among aggressive AI-native robotics startups -- a leading indicator to watch for when these techniques cross into production use.
insights.quote: there are definitely drawbacks to using that and there's ways it could could go wrong

themes.theme: Mogul's Fractional Real Estate Investor Economics

insights.claim: Mogul, founded by ex-Goldman Sachs real estate private equity staff whose co-founder scaled Goldman's own single-family rental platform from zero to $1 billion in under 12 months with a team of three to four people, lets retail investors buy fractional equity shares in individual single-family rental homes for a $250 minimum, though the average check size is actually $15,000-$20,000 with 20-80 investors per property.
insights.speaker: Alex Blackwood, founder/CEO of Mogul
insights.so_what: Despite marketing a low $250 entry point, the real investor base skews toward five-figure checks -- useful context for assessing Mogul's actual customer profile versus its retail-democratization pitch.
insights.quote: my co-founder grew Goldman single family rental platform from zero to a billion in under 12 months with three to four individuals

insights.claim: Mogul has completed 130 property acquisitions totaling over 600 rental units, with individual property values typically ranging from $500,000 to $2.5 million, and targets a blended investor return of roughly 15-20% annually (8-12% cash dividend yield plus 3-4% underlying appreciation amplified to 4-8% via mortgage leverage), while underwriting deals conservatively to a 12% IRR bare-case scenario.
insights.speaker: Alex Blackwood
insights.so_what: A stated 15-20% blended target return with monthly cash distribution is a concrete benchmark investors can compare against public REITs or direct rental ownership.
insights.quote: the assets typically yield between 8 to 12% per year for dividend payout

insights.claim: Mogul structures each property purchase with an upfront maintenance reserve sized to the property's equipment lifecycle plus a full 12-month vacancy reserve, so that investors are never subject to a capital call even in a worst-case prolonged vacancy or major repair like a $67,000 HVAC replacement.
insights.speaker: Alex Blackwood
insights.so_what: Eliminating capital-call risk is a structural differentiator versus typical real estate syndications, reducing downside surprise risk for passive retail investors -- worth verifying against Mogul's actual reserve adequacy over a full market cycle.
insights.quote: we actually capitalize a 12 month reserve. So that asset could sit completely vacant for up to 12 months and we would never call another dollar in the door

insights.claim: Mogul investors receive a tax benefit where the 8-12% annual rental income yield is largely or entirely offset for tax-reporting purposes by real estate depreciation, which Blackwood calls a 'ghost expense' since it reduces taxable income without reducing the investor's actual cash flow.
insights.speaker: Alex Blackwood
insights.so_what: Depreciation-shielded rental yield materially changes after-tax return comparisons between Mogul and other yield-generating assets like bonds or REIT dividends, which are typically taxed as ordinary income.
insights.quote: even though you're generating that 8 to 12% yield, the depreciation should cover it up and then some

insights.claim: Mogul currently has no true liquidity mechanism: investors are told to expect a 5-7 year hold period per property with no way to exit early, comparable to the illiquidity of startup SPV investing that the host contrasts it with.
insights.speaker: Alex Blackwood
insights.so_what: Illiquidity is a key risk factor for retail investors sold on the $250-minimum, 'headache-free' pitch -- worth flagging prominently against the return numbers when evaluating the product.
insights.quote: today it's a 5 to sevenyear typical period

themes.theme: Mogul's Revenue Model, Liquidity Roadmap & Market Selection

insights.claim: Mogul monetizes each deal through a 5% upfront platform fee on purchase price (amortized like roughly 1% per year of drag over a 5-year hold), plus a separate 1-1.5% fee paid by the property seller at closing when Mogul buys direct without a broker, plus roughly 2.75% effective interest earned on investors' maintenance and vacancy reserve cash.
insights.speaker: Alex Blackwood
insights.so_what: The seller-paid fee and reserve interest are revenue streams that don't directly reduce investor returns, meaning Mogul's effective take-rate is higher than the visible 5% fee alone suggests -- important for modeling platform unit economics.
insights.quote: we charge a platform fee up front. It's 5% charge as percentage of purchase price

insights.claim: Mogul is launching new ancillary revenue lines including a title insurance joint venture (adding roughly $9,000-$12,000 per asset) by the end of the current quarter and a property insurance underwriting business, targeting roughly $4-5 million in recurring revenue in year one from these new verticals while also improving cash flow to investors via better underwriting data.
insights.speaker: Alex Blackwood
insights.so_what: Vertical integration into title and insurance is a classic proptech margin-expansion playbook; the stated dollar figures give investors a near-term revenue catalyst to track for verification.

insights.claim: Mogul plans to launch a secondary market for its fractional property shares next year, with Mogul itself acting as the day-one market maker (taking a bid-ask spread and using rental income to cover potential debt service), aiming to create what Blackwood describes as a fully liquid secondary market that has never previously existed for this asset class.
insights.speaker: Alex Blackwood
insights.so_what: A functioning secondary market would meaningfully de-risk the illiquidity problem in fractional real estate investing and could become a competitive differentiator -- a milestone worth monitoring for launch confirmation.
insights.quote: we're looking to do what's never been done before, which is a fully liquid secondary market

insights.claim: Mogul selects target markets by analyzing whether projected new housing supply is insufficient to meet demand and whether the rent-to-price ratio is dislocated enough to generate attractive yields; it cites Austin as a current example, where a 2020-2022 supply surge drove prices down roughly 25% but new development has since pulled back sharply, creating a more favorable future supply-demand setup despite four consecutive years of falling rents and prices.
insights.speaker: Alex Blackwood
insights.so_what: A contrarian 'buy where builders just got scared away' framework -- Austin's recent price weakness is framed as a forward-looking buying opportunity rather than a red flag, a thesis investors in single-family rental strategies can independently check against Austin permitting/inventory data.
insights.quote: net new supply on the horizon is not enough to meet demand
